narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 39 (above the average 36.70), Year 1 school decile of 2 (much lower than the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 1 (well below the average 5.53), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 72.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Most critically, your very low Year 3 decile—placing you in the bottom tier of your school's grades that year—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a significant drop-off in performance at a key stage. In contrast, your solid LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward a higher chance of passing by demonstrating strong analytical potential compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward the failure prediction, as being in the lower ranks early on reinforced concerns about consistent academic strength. Overall, while your LSAT stands out positively against typical students, your weaker decile rankings in both early and later years outweighed it, setting you apart from applicants who maintain higher school-relative performance throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your GPA and decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or community college refreshers, and reapply after building a stronger academic trajectory. You might also seek bar prep resources early or explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your LSAT more heavily. With focused effort on those early academic hurdles, you can shift future predictions in your favor.
